coredata.dc:title: Carrageenan-based active and intelligent packaging films integrated with anthocyanin and TiO2-doped carbon dots derived from sweet potato peels
coredata.dc:creator: Riahi, Zohreh
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: Carrageenan-based sustainable active and pH-dependent color-changing composite films were fabricated by blending anthocyanin extracted from sweet potato peel (SPA) with TiO2-doped carbon dots (Ti-CDs) prepared using the biowaste of SPA extraction. The SPA and Ti-CDs were compatible with the carrageenan matrix and were uniformly dispersed in the used polymer to form a homogeneous film with increased mechanical properties. The composite film added with SPA and 3 wt% Ti-CD showed 100 % UV protection, superb antioxidant (100 % DPPH and ABTS scavenging assay), and potent antibacterial activity (complete eradication of foodborne L. monocytogenes and E. coli strains after 3 h incubation). Additionally, the composite films showed distinguishable colorimetric responses to pH 7–12 buffers and volatile ammonia. The intelligent sensing ability of the composite film was assessed through shrimp freshness monitoring, and the film's hue shifted from pink (fresh shrimp) to yellow/brown (inedible shrimp) during storage. Shrimp packaging studies have shown that composite films retard the rate of food quality change during storage and are a good indicator of shrimp spoilage. Therefore, the designed film is expected to have high applicability as a chip, and quick on-site sensor that detects seafood quality in real-time, and a highly effective multifunctional film for better product quality preservation.

head.ce:title: Carrageenan-based active and intelligent packaging films integrated with anthocyanin and TiO2-doped carbon dots derived from sweet potato peels

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carrageenan-based sustainable active and pH-dependent color-changing composite films were fabricated by blending anthocyanin extracted from sweet potato peel (SPA) with TiO2-doped carbon dots (Ti-CDs) prepared using the biowaste of SPA extraction. The SPA and Ti-CDs were compatible with the carrageenan matrix and were uniformly dispersed in the used polymer to form a homogeneous film with increased mechanical properties. The composite film added with SPA and 3 wt% Ti-CD showed 100 % UV protection, superb antioxidant (100 % DPPH and ABTS scavenging assay), and potent antibacterial activity (complete eradication of foodborne L. monocytogenes and E. coli strains after 3 h incubation). Additionally, the composite films showed distinguishable colorimetric responses to pH 7–12 buffers and volatile ammonia. The intelligent sensing ability of the composite film was assessed through shrimp freshness monitoring, and the film's hue shifted from pink (fresh shrimp) to yellow/brown (inedible shrimp) during storage. Shrimp packaging studies have shown that composite films retard the rate of food quality change during storage and are a good indicator of shrimp spoilage. Therefore, the designed film is expected to have high applicability as a chip, and quick on-site sensor that detects seafood quality in real-time, and a highly effective multifunctional film for better product quality preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditionally, food packaging has been known to play an imperative role in preserving and distributing food within the supply chain by providing an effective barrier against moisture, oxygen, and mechanical stress [1]. Evolving consumer lifestyles fuel a growing demand for secure, superior-quality, fresh, and minimally processed products. Consequently, modernized packaging technologies are desperately needed to meet these requirements [2,3]. Innovative technologies such as intelligent and active packaging are progressively being explored in the food sector. However, a majority of them are still in the research stage and have not yet been fully commercialized [4,5].

Active packaging is the intentional inclusion of materials that interact with the packaging's internal environment while preserving the safety and quality aspects of the packaged products [6]. On the other hand, intelligent packaging entails the integration of materials sensitive to the packaging environment and continuously monitors various aspects of the storage conditions and shelf life of the stored food. This kind of packaging can detect and warn of possible spoilage or potential problems with the packaged food [3].

Functional plant extracts, raw food materials, nanomaterials, and essential oils were added as active ingredients to improve the antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV-blocking performance of the composite film [7–11]. Carbon dots (CDs), recently emerging as novel carbon-based nanomaterials, have been the concern of many research efforts in recent years [12]. CDs offer beneficial features such as good water solubility, tunable functional groups, low toxicity, and antibacterial/antioxidant activity, making them promising active fillers in the packaging industry [13]. A viable option for these CDs is an easy and sustainable synthesis process using cost-effective, safe, and environmentally friendly methods from various biomass/waste sources, including food and agricultural waste, polysaccharides, proteins, biopolymers, and microorganisms [14].

However, the tendency of biomass-derived CDs to aggregate without effective stabilizers hinders their antioxidant and antimicrobial functions. In this regard, a viable strategy to achieve stable and functional CDs is the incorporation of metal nanoparticles through doping [15]. CDs possess a chemical structure comprising a central carbon core and surface functional groups. The physicochemical characteristics of CDs may vary depending on the chemical structure of CDs and the specific functional moieties present in the carbon source [16]. Incorporating heteroatoms and metal doping within the core chemical structure of CDs can acquire new functions and broaden the range of applications. In this aspect, doping CDs with TiO2 nanoparticles (Ti-NPs) can be a good strategy to obtain stable CDs with excellent functional properties. Through doping, numerous COOH and OH groups on the CD surface bind with Ti-NPs to stabilize the final structure [15]. Nevertheless, the utilization of metal-doped CDs to improve food safety is still in its infancy. Therefore, further studies are needed to develop highly efficient and stable CDs that can serve as active ingredients.

However, these bioactive packaging films have limitations in the timely monitoring of food freshness, making it difficult for consumers to select products according to the desired freshness [17]. Accordingly, there is an increasing need for the creation of active/intelligent dual-functional (smart) packaging films that effectively maintain food quality and enable real-time monitoring [18]. Currently, the ability to rapidly detect changes in food conditions has given noteworthy appeal to ingenious packaging systems that rely on pH-responsive color-changing substances [19]. Anthocyanins, natural colorants, are commonly used as halochromic sensors in smart packaging because they change color in response to different external factors such as pH, gas level, and temperature [20,21]. Intelligent color indicator films have been developed using anthocyanins sourced from diverse origins [22,23]. The performance of films is motivated by items such as source, amount, and purity of anthocyanins, film preparation methods and storage conditions [4,24]. Sweet potato peel is an interesting and rich source of acylated anthocyanins, which is suitable for developing discoloration films due to its high nutritional value [25]. In addition, anthocyanin extracted from sweet potato peel has excellent stability to temperature and light compared to other plant-derived colorants [26].

Biopolymers (proteins, lipids, and polysaccharides) are employed as carriers to combine with functional ingredients, enabling the development of environmentally friendly, multifunctional, active, and intelligent composite films [22]. Among polysaccharides, carrageenan (Car) can be considered one of the most efficient polymer materials suitable for food packaging purposes due to its abundance, environmental friendliness, and renewable potential [27]. The marine polysaccharide κ-carrageenan is a linear sulfated galactan family member isolated from red algae [28]. However, the scarcity of favorable physical characteristics such as mechanical, moisture barrier, and functional characteristics has limited carrageenan's industrial use [29].

This study aimed to create a new sustainable colorimetric functional film with the potential for food preservation by combining biowaste-derived carbon dots and anthocyanin with carrageenan film. Carbon dots were employed as active agents that present functional capabilities such as antibacterial and antioxidant activity, whereas anthocyanins were used as pH-sensitive indicators. Moreover, the electrostatic interaction between rich-functional groups of carbon dots with polymer matrix and anthocyanin can improve the adsorption and stability of anthocyanin in polymer chains, intensifying the gas and pH sensing performance of the indicator film. To this end, a carrageenan-based multifunctional composite film was developed using anthocyanin (SPA) extracted from the sweet potato peels and TiO2-doped carbon dots (Ti-CDs) derived from biowaste (leftover after SPA extraction). The designed films were characterized and evaluated for their functional capabilities. In addition, the pH-responsive color change of the films was analyzed. Next, the efficacy of the engineered film for the smart packaging applications was monitored using a shrimp packaging test.

κ-Carrageenan (Car) (aqueous solution of 1.5 % at 75 °C with a viscosity of 50 cp and molecular weight of 1.71 × 106 g/mol, HE-A) was supplied by Hankook Carrageenan (Jeonnam, South Korea). Titanium dioxide (anatase, particle size <25 nm) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 23235), Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895), and Salmonella enterica (ATCC 14028) were obtained from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC). Fresh shrimp and sweet potatoes were obtained from a local supermarket in Seoul, Korea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sweet potato anthocyanin and Ti-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Anthocyanin was extracted from sweet potato peel (SPA), adopting a method described by Kan et al. [30]. 100 g of dry sweet potato peel powder immersed in 1000 mL of water/ethanol mixture (1:4 ratio) with mechanically agitating at 25 °C overnight. The extracted solution was filtered, followed by concentrating the supernatant via a rotary evaporator, and the SPA was stored in a refrigerator for the next analysis. The calculation of the anthocyanin amount in the extracted solution is described in the Supporting Information.

The biowaste residue of sweet potato peels after SPA extraction was further utilized to synthesize carbon dots using a one-step hydrothermal approach. For this, the precipitant of anthocyanin extraction was dried at 40 °C. The dried powder (1 g) and 5 wt% of TiO2 nanoparticles were mixed with distilled water (50 mL). The mixture was shifted into a stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and kept at 190 °C for 6 h (Fig. 1 ). After cooling to 30 °C, brown supernatant solution was obtained via centrifuging at 1000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained TiO2-doped CDs (Ti-CDs) solution was filtrated using the Whatman filter membrane (0.22 μm) and left at 4 °C before further investigations. Details of the characterization of the Ti-CDs are depicted in the Supporting Information.

A carrageenan solution was provided by dissolving 4 g of Car in 200 mL of distilled water under strong mechanical agitation for 1 h at 90 °C while adding glycerol (1.2 g). The solution was cooled to 55 °C, and 10 mL ethanolic sweet potato peel extracted solution (50 mg extract/mL) SPA was mixed with stirring for 15 min. Then, 1 and 3 wt% of Ti-CDs were mixed into the prepared solution with mild stirring for 20 min. The solutions were cast onto a glass plate (Teflon-coated, size: 30 cm × 24 cm) followed by drying at 25 °C for 48 h. The dried films were conditioned in a 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity (RH) environment until further testing. For comparison, two more films (the pristine carrageenan and SPA-loaded carrageenan films) were engineered by adopting the same approach. The constructed films were denoted as Car, Car/SPA, Car/SPA/Ti-CD1%, and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3%, according to the type and concentration of fillers. Methods for determining the film's functional and physicochemical characteristics are explained in Supporting Information.

The gas detection capability of the proposed film was evaluated by attaching a film sample (3 cm × 3 cm) onto a beaker attaining 30 mL of 99 % acetic acid or 0.8 M ammonia for 15 min. The film's color determination was assessed through a chromameter (Konica-Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan).

A film specimen (circular disk with a diameter of 3 cm) was affixed to the inner surface of a packaging box comprising 100 g of shrimp pieces. After that, the sealed container was stored at 25 ± 2 °C for 1 day. The color values of the fabricated film were observed at a duration of 12 h using a chroma meter to monitor shrimp freshness. In addition, TVB-N (total volatile basic nitrogen) concentrations and pH shifting of shrimp were assessed. After digesting 5 g of the stored shrimp with 0.1 % peptone water (45 mL), the pH of the obtained solution was assessed through a digital pH meter. TVB-N concentrations were estimated via the steam distillation technique. TVB-N was obtained by a Kjeldahl distillation apparatus, stored in a flask, and titrated with sulfuric acid (0.1 N) to determine the concentration of released TVB-N. TVB-N concentrations are listed as mg/100 g sample (mg %).

A packaging experiment was applied to study the film's dual ability to extend the shelf life of shrimp while monitoring freshness in real-time. For this, fresh shrimp samples (25 g) were packaged in pre-sterilized individual carrageenan-based film (10 cm × 10 cm) and kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator. The samples were investigated every 5 days to determine the quality changes (color, pH, and total bacterial count). The package's visual appearance was confirmed using a digital camera. Shrimp samples (5 g) were mixed with sterilized water (45 mL) in a stomacher bag and homogenized for 3 min in a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). Subsequently, the pH values of the obtained digested samples were measured using a digital pH meter. Additionally, 1 mL of diluted homogenate was spread onto 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates, and TVC (total viable cell count) was assessed to determine the bacterial population of packaged specimens. The procedure was carried out in triplicate, presenting the results as average values.

All the measurements were repeated in three determinations and listed the data as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was carried out, and the implication of each mean value was evaluated (p < 0.05) by Duncan's multiple range test through the statistical investigation (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) software.

The morphology, chemical structure, and optical characteristics of the Ti-CDs were evaluated by TEM, FTIR, and UV absorption, and the results are depicted in Fig. 2 . The Ti-CDs have a spherical morphology in the quantum range with an 8.2 nm average diameter and a uniform distribution with high monodispersity (Fig. 2a,b).

The FTIR spectrum of Ti-CDs depicted a broad band at 3180–3385 cm−1, assigning to the stretching mode of O-H/N-H groups (Fig. 2c). The absorption peak at 1704 cm−1, sharp bands located around 1399 and 1020 cm−1 indicated the vibration of CO, CN, and C-O-C, respectively [31,32]. The peaks around ~2926 and ~ 1238 cm−1 can be attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CH and CH2 bonds present in methyl and methylene groups. The transmittance peak at 1123 cm−1 displayed the C-O-H bonds stretching vibration [33]. Further, 1447 and 772 cm−1 peaks indicated the C-O-Ti and TiO bond formation [34,35], verifying the successful doping of carbon dots with TiO2. The FTIR outcomes validated the abundance of OH, amide, and carboxylic groups on Ti-CDs.

The UV–vis light spectrum of the Ti-CDs solution depicts a sharp band at 285 nm, indicating the CO group (n-π* transition) (Fig. 2d) [31]. The Ti-CDs were a brownish solution in sunlight, and a blue fluorescent emission was noticed under UV light (365 nm), indicating high FL emission of the fabricated carbon dots (Fig. 2(d), inset). The quantum confinement effect leads to an instantaneous change in color when exposed to UV radiation, a characteristic phenomenon of CDs. The synthesis route and choice of carbon precursor affect the intensity of the emitted color [36].

FL emission spectra were recorded at 10 nm increments of excitation wavelength from 290 to 440 nm (Fig. 2e). Emission data illustrated that Ti-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent phenomenon with clear red shift when excited under longer wavelength [37]. The Ti-CDs displayed maximum peak fluorescence emission at 437 nm under optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2d). The tunable FL emission of CDs is impacted by several factors such as carbon sources, changes in size and shape, and the presence of distinct luminescent sites on the surface of the CDs [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial activity of Ti-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Free radicals are very reactive molecules that may oxidatively damage various food components, leading to their spoilage. Antioxidants neutralize these free radicals and protect food from oxidative stress, maintaining food quality and extending its shelf life [39]. The antioxidant capacity of Ti-CDs was measured using ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging capacity, and the results are depicted in Fig. 2f. The Ti-CDs showed dose-dependent antioxidant activity. The Ti-CDs showed 38.5 % and 63.4 % of antioxidant activities against DPPH and ABTS at 12.5 μg/mL, respectively. As the Ti-CDs concentration increased, the scavenging performance for ABTS and DPPH radicals increased significantly (p < 0.05), showing 100 % ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging ability at 50 μg/mL concentration. H-atom donation from the edge site functional groups such as -OH, -NH2, and -COOH has induced the radical capturing capacity of CDs [40]. The DPPH and ABTS molecules consist of an N atom with a lone pair of electrons surrounded by three benzene rings that accept hydrogen shifts and react with Ti-CDs. This reaction forms ABTS-H and DPPH-H complexes, changing color from cyan and deep purple tones to colorless and yellow shades, respectively [40]. In addition, the contribution of CDs to anti-radical activity is facilitated through electron donation, leading to the formation of adducts. The redox function of CDs plays an important contribution in neutralizing free radicals, quenching singlet and triplet oxygen, or breaking down superoxide [41]. The electron and proton transfer properties of CDs can be influenced and amplified by adding metal dopants, augmenting their antioxidant function [40,42].

Antibacterial active packaging inhibits the growth and proliferation of bacteria on the surface of food products, improving food safety and storage time and reducing the likelihood of foodborne illnesses [43]. The MIC/MBC and well diffusion strategy was adopted to examine the antibacterial efficacy of Ti-CDs towards E. coli and S. enterica (Gram-negative) and L. monocytogenes and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) as the common food pathogenic bacteria. The Ti-CDs showed MIC/MBC values of 100/200 μg/mL and 200/300 μg/mL against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus, where a value of 400/500 μg/mL and 200/300 μg/mL was observed against E. coli and S. enterica. In addition, the qualitative evaluation of the antibacterial activity (Fig. 2g) is consistent with the MIC findings, confirming the strong inhibitory effect of Ti-CDs against all tested strains. Consequently, the hydrophilic Ti-CDs exhibit swift diffusion and dispersion within the agar medium, leading to a bactericidal effect [44].

Several hypotheses have been put forward to elucidate the mechanism of the outstanding antibacterial effect of carbon dots. These hypotheses consider various factors, including the electrostatic interactions, small size, surface functional groups of CDs, and the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Electrostatic interactions and chemical conjugations allow Ti-CDs to attach to the bacterial cell membrane, causing physical/mechanical damage and bacterial cell wall disruption [45]. The hydrogen and amide bonding on the surface of Ti-CDs interacts electrostatically with the peptidoglycans, proteins, and phospholipid of the bacterial somatic membrane, resulting in the breakdown of the bio-membrane and release of the intracellular components [40,46]. Their size strongly influences the distribution of nano-sized Ti-CDs within the membrane structure, and the bacteria cell wall is permeable to the small nanoparticles. The ultra-small CDs can easily pass through microorganisms' cell walls and disturb cell actions, destroying cells. The generation of ROS, including singlet oxygen, hydroxyl free radicals, and superoxide anions, is another important antibacterial mechanism that might cause oxidative stress to upset cellular homeostasis and damage cellular processes [47].

In addition, metal ions dopant (such as Ti ions) in CDs augments their antibacterial activity by facilitating electron transfer processes and amplifying the ROS production rate [48]. Likewise, various metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+, can induce ROS production through chemical reactions within cells [46]. The generated ROS can disintegrate proteins and nucleic acids and cause peroxidation of lipids [49].

The surface morphology of the Car-based films and distribution of SPA and different Ti-CD contents were depicted in Fig. 3a,b using the FE-SEM images. The surface of the pure Car film displayed a uniform, smooth, and free void morphology. The addition of SPA did not change the film's surface due to the good dispersion in the Car matrix, indicating the good compatibility of SPA with the carrageenan polymer. On the other hand, after adding 1 wt% Ti-CDs, the microstructure of the film appeared slightly rougher than the Car film. Although the film surface of Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% showed some lumps, the consistent distribution of Ti-CDs throughout the polymer is deduced from the SEM images. The good compatibility and intramolecular interactions of added fillers with the carrageenan matrix impart a homogeneous and dense microstructure without any phase separation to developed nanocomposite films. The composite films presented compact and homogeneous cross-sections without pore formation, suggesting a good distribution of SPA and Ti-CDs within the Car matrix (Fig. 3b). Consequently, the integration of SPA and Ti-CDs fillers into the carrageenan films exhibiting distinctive shapes and structures, proposing the possibility of improvements in the physical characteristics of the composite films.

The EDX elemental mappings of Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film are shown in Fig. 3c. The structural backbone of the carrageenan polymer consists of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur elements. The additional peaks of the nitrogen and titanium were observed in the EDX spectra, confirming the successful integration of Ti-NPs doped nitrogen-rich carbon dots into the Car polymer matrix. Moreover, the elemental mapping analysis of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film indicated a uniform distribution of individual elements across the surface, signifying a well-dispersed presence of Ti-CDs throughout the carrageenan matrix.

The FT-IR results of the provided films loaded with Ti-CDs nanoparticles and SPA are illustrated in Fig. 3d. All prepared films exhibited a broad peak at around 3328 cm−1, corresponding to the OH group. The 2936 and 2883 cm−1 peaks refer to stretching vibrations of the CH bond in the carrageenan polymer chain. The transmittance peaks at 1641 cm−1 and 1223 cm−1 can correspond to the presence of amide I and ester sulfate group (O=S=O) in the carrageenan [28]. The 3,6-anhydro-D-galactose and O-SO3 of galactose-4-sulfate characteristic peaks were observed at 916 and 842cm−1, respectively [27]. After adding Ti-CDs into the carrageenan, a small peak at 1743 cm−1 related to the vibration of carbon dots' CO groups stretching was also observed, confirming the good dispersion of the nanofillers into the polymer matrix. Overall, no notable alterations in functional groups were observed in the nanocomposite films, depicting that the Car backbone remained unchanged in the carbohydrate region (700–1700 cm−1) after blending with nanoparticles [27]. Therefore, the good miscibility between Car and fillers was due to the formation of strong intermolecular interactions within the film matrix, reflected by some of the peaks (3328 cm−1) in the composite films.

XPS analyzed the chemical state of fillers in the carrageenan matrix, and the full survey and deconvoluted spectra of neat Car and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films are shown in Fig. 4a-f. The XPS full-scan spectrum of the Car-based film confirms the major core levels of C 1 s, S 2p, and O 1S at binding energies of ~283.2 eV, 168.3 eV, and 530.5 eV, respectively. However, the additional N 1 s and Ti 2p peaks around ~398.0 and ~ 460 eV indicate the successful loading of Ti-CDs into the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% composite film (Fig. 4a). The deconvoluted XPS C 1 s spectrum showed that the C 1 s peaks of the Car and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% could be decomposed into C-C/C-H (284.4 eV), C-N/C=C (285.3eV), C-S/C-O (286.7eV), and (CO or O-C-O) (288.0eV) components, respectively (Fig. 4b). The high-resolution S 2p spectrum of both films (Fig. 4c) could be divided into three peaks centered around binding energies of 167.4 eV (C-S-O), 168.2 eV (-OSO3 2− (S 2p3/2)), and 169 eV (-OSO3 2− (S 2p1/2)), respectively [50,51]. Three peaks around 530.4, 531.3, and 532.1 eV appeared in both films' high-resolution O 1 s spectra, which could be attributed to Ti-O/C=O, C-O-H/C-O-C, and O-C-O groups, respectively (Fig. 4d) [52]. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra of Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% (Fig. 4e) exhibited three characteristic peaks at 398.0 eV (C-N-C), 398.9eV (N-(C)3), and 399.6 eV (NH2 group) [51]. Also, Fig. 4f shows two peaks at 457.1 and 466.7 eV, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, indicating the Ti4+ state of the Ti-CDs [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparent and optical characteristics of Car/SPA/Ti-CD films
ce:section.ce:para: The actual hue of the engineered Car-based films is depicted in Fig. 5a. The virgin carrageenan film had no color and was very transparent. After the addition of SPA, the film exhibited a pink color but was still transparent. Adding 1 and 3 wt% Ti-CDs changes the film's color to dark pink and reddish because of the synergetic effect of SPA's purple/pink color and the brownish color of Ti-CDs. The enhanced colorant adsorption facilitated by the high surface area of these ultra-small carbon dots contributes to a synergistic effect when blending the two colors, resulting in a darker color. The nanocomposite films displayed slick coloration, suggesting a uniform distribution of anthocyanin and carbon dots within the polymer matrix.

Many food products are sensitive to UV radiation, as it can lead to the degradation of nutrients, changes in flavor, color fading, and reduced shelf life. The light-barrier properties of a packaging film play a crucial role in safeguarding foods from photochemical reactions [54]. The light transmittance of the Car-based films at 200–800 nm was recorded to assess their transparency and UV-barrier efficacy (Fig. 5b). The UV–vis spectra displayed that the pure Car film was transparent to visible light and UV light. On the other hand, the absorption profile of the composite film exhibited distinct dissimilarity due to the UV and visible light absorption of SPA and Ti-CDs. The films containing SPA and Ti-CDs exhibited visible adsorption peaks at ~550 nm due to hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between the added compound and carrageenan matrix [1]. Table 1 shows the results of the examination of the film's UV-light barrier properties and transparency using absorbance measurements at 280 nm and 660 nm, respectively. The neat Car film showed 90.5 % and 61.5 % of the T660 and T280, respectively. The addition of SPA significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the T280 and T660 of the Car-based film. Notably, T280 decreased more significantly (by 87.8 % reduction) than T660 (by 2.8 % reduction) due to UV absorption by polyphenolic compounds rich in aromatic groups of anthocyanins [55]. Incorporating Ti-CDs further significantly (p < 0.05) reduced T280, exhibiting ~100 % UV absorption properties by adding 1 wt% and 3 wt% of Ti-CDs. At the same time, the transparency remains ~83.9 % and ~ 79.4 % for the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% composite films, which are suitable for packaging applications. These results indicate that the combined addition of SPA and Ti-CDs imparted UV protection properties to the Car-based film, ensuring food stability and prolonging the shelf life of high-fat products. Previous research has similarly highlighted a notable reduction in light transmittance of polymer films due to CDs, attributed to their UV light absorption ability [44,51].

The color of the film influences consumer acceptance of packaged foods [34]. Table 1 shows the L, a, b , and ΔE values of the Car-based films. The neat Car film was bright with an L-value of 91.2 ± 0.1. The addition of SPA significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the brightness and enhanced redness (increased a-value) and yellowness (increased b-value), resulting in a significantly (p < 0.05) increased total color difference (ΔE). The results suggest the anthocyanins could interact with the polymer matrix through different molecular interactions. The L-value of Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films decreased while the a- and b-values increased. Compared to the neat Car film, the ΔE values of the CD-added composite films were significantly higher (p < 0.05) than the Car/SPA films, suggesting that the Car/SPA/Ti-CDs composite films were more pigmented. The color change was mainly due to the selective absorption of light by the incorporated CDs [36,37]. The increase in nanoparticle concentration caused the scattering effects, diminishing the transmission of light through the films and subsequently reducing the lightness [56]. The enhanced a- and b-values are due to the combined effect of the color of SPA and Ti-CDs.

Packaging materials with appropriate mechanical properties protect the food products. They should have sufficient strength and durability to withstand handling, transportation, and storage without damage or compromise [57]. The mechanical strength of the films was evaluated by measuring tensile strength (TS), while the toughness and rigidity of the films were characterized by measuring elongation at break (EB) and elastic modulus (EM), respectively (Table 2 ). The thickness of the neat carrageenan film was 47.8 ± 1.4 μm, which increased significantly with the addition of SPA and different concentrations of Ti-CDs. This effect corresponds to an increase in the solids content of the film, which increases its thickness by pushing the polymer chains further. The Car film was strong, with TS of ~90.9 MPa. The addition of SPA significantly decreased TS & EM and increased EB, indicating that added SPA acts as a plasticizer. It is well known that anthocyanins act as plasticizers, breaking secondary bonds and making polymer molecules easier to move [58]. The reduced strength has been frequently observed in various biopolymer films by adding natural colorants [55,59,60]. However, adding 1 and 3 wt% Ti-CDs increased the TS to ~95.8 and ~ 105.6 MPa, respectively. The formation of intramolecular and intermolecular H-bonding between the functional groups of Ti-CDs and hydroxyl groups of the base polymer is responsible for the strengthening effect of the nanofillers, enhancing the breaking energy requirement. The flexibility of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films was lower than the anthocyanin-added film but greater than the neat carrageenan film. The incorporation of sweet potato anthocyanin decreased the stiffness of the carrageenan film. However, when SPA and Ti-CDs were added together, the rigidity was increased again, and there was a statistically significant difference from the control film. This may be due to the interactions between the two types of fillers and the overall interaction with the base polymer, which leads to increased compatibility and compactness of the film structure, resulting in increased stiffness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: WVP is an essential measure for biopolymer-based films as it shows how well the films can control the passage of water vapor between food and its surroundings [61]. The less moisture that is transferred from the food to the surrounding environment, the better the film is considered to be. Since WVP comes into close contact with food objects and has a significant impact on their shelf life, it is one of the essential elements of bio-composite films used in food packaging. Therefore, before performing the WVP test, the Car/SPA/Ti-CD films were conditioned in a relative humidity (RH) chamber at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 2 h so that the moisture content of the films stabilized. The water resistance properties of the prepared composite films were also summarized in Table 2. The neat Car film has a WVP of 8.96 × 10−10 g·m/m2·Pa·s, which is increased to 12.90 ± 0.01 g·m/m2·Pa·s by the addition of SPA. The large aromatic ring structure of anthocyanins hindered the molecular force between polymer and SPA, and the dense network structure of the films became loose, thereby expanding internal molecular free volume and promoting the movement of molecular chains. Consequently, water vapor penetration through the polymer film will increase [62]. The water vapor transition of the film was significantly decreased by adding 1 and 3 wt% Ti-CDs compared to the Car/SPA. Incorporating nanofillers can occupy the interchain voids, forming a compact network structure that increases tortuosity. This can improve the effectiveness of the films as barriers against water vapor by impeding the diffusion of water vapor [63].

The WCA of the carrageenan film was 61.4°, while a significant (p < 0.05) reduction in WCA was found after adding SPA (Table 2). The WCA increased from 45.3° (for Car/SPA) to 60.7° (for Car/SPA/Ti-CD1%). A possible reason is that the hydrogen bonding between SPA and Ti-CDs and their interaction with the polymer matrix reduces the available hydrophilic free hydroxyl groups, reducing hydrophilicity [51]. However, the increase of Ti-CDs concentration to 3 wt% decreased the WCA to 55.1°, probably due to the high concentration of the hydrophilic Ti-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The radical scavenging performance of the Car-based films was determined using ABTS and DPPH methods. As evident from Fig. 6a, the antioxidant efficacy of the neat Car film was negligible in both ABTS (1.3 %) and DPPH (0.5 %) methods. The Car/SPA film showed radical scavenging activity of 29.4 % in ABTS and 16.1 % in DPPH. Anthocyanin possesses abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups that are rich in electrons. These electron-rich groups enable anthocyanin to readily donate electrons and hydrogen atoms to reactive free radicals, effectively neutralizing them [64]. The DPPH• and ABTS•+ scavenging action of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% film was 81.1 ± 0.8 % and 100 %, respectively. In particular, the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film quenched ABTS and DPPH radicals completely. The color of the DPPH solution shifted from deep violet to light yellow, while the ABTS solution changed from cyan blue to colorless. The enhanced antioxidant efficacy of the CDs-added composite films is believed to be due to the functional groups located on the Ti-CDs' surface. These functional groups possess a strong hydrogen supply capacity, allowing hydrogen to react with fatty acid-free radicals, transforming the free radicals into more stable products, thereby interrupting the chain reaction of free radicals and preventing the automatic oxidation of products [62]. Therefore, the engineered Car-based composite films can serve as packaging materials with antioxidant properties materials, effectively scavenging free radicals and delaying lipid oxidation in food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial potential of Car-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes is depicted in Fig. 6b. The neat Car film did not show any antibacterial effect against test pathogen bacteria, and the film's surface had signs of bacterial colonies. The SPA-added film showed a reduced growth rate against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, showing 2.1 Log CFU/mL and 1.6 Log CFU/mL lower than the control group at 12 h of incubation, respectively. In contrast, Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films showed bactericidal effects against both kinds of bacteria, eradicating the L. monocytogenes and E. coli after 9 h and 3 h of incubation, respectively. It is believed that adding Ti-CDs protects prepared nanocomposite films from contamination by foodborne pathogens, maintaining the films' antibacterial properties during the food packaging period. The superior antibacterial efficacy of the composite film can result from the antibacterial performance of Ti-CDs, as explained in Section 3.1.3. Meanwhile, active SPA and released Ti-ions can interact with intracellular proteins and stop bacterial reproduction and growth [65]. Notably, the lower bactericidal rate observed in the active film against E. coli can be attributed to the presence of the lipopolysaccharide membrane in the E. coli cell wall, which acts as a barrier and hinders the penetration of active Ti-CDs and SPA into the cell membrane [66].

Several studies have reported that the incorporation of CDs into different biopolymers-based composite films, such as CNF [67], CMC [44], and carrageenan/alginate films [51], imparts notable antioxidant and antibacterial activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color change of SPA and indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7a shows the color change of SPA in various buffer solutions between pH 2–12. The extracted SPA (anthocyanin content: 232 mg of cyanidin-3-glucoside equivalent/g extract) exhibited a wide range of color changes ranging from red at pH 2–6, purple at pH 7–9, blue at pH 10, and green/yellow at pH 11–12 [68]. This color change is due to structural changes undergone by anthocyanin in different pH solutions where it exhibits a red flavylium cationic structure at pH 2–6, a purple/blue quinoidal anhydrobase at pH 7–10, green chalcone and yellow chalcone at pH 11–12 [69].

Further, it is well known that natural colorants are temperature-sensitive and generally not stable under higher temperatures. Chen et al. [70] investigated the thermal stability of sweet potato anthocyanin at different temperatures (4 °C, 25 °C, 37 °C, and 55 °C). According to their report, storage at 4 °C and 25 °C did not result in a significant change in color appearance and the change of anthocyanin content was <5 %. In contrast, the color appearance and anthocyanin content changed significantly at 37 °C and 55 °C. Therefore, to realize the exact performance of the proposed smart film, all the experiments, such as freshness monitoring and packaging test, were performed at or below room temperature (25 °C). Herein, SPA-containing films exhibited similar color changes to anthocyanin solutions in different pH buffer solutions. The pH indicator ability of Car-based films is presented in Fig. 7b. The color of the Car/SPA film was pale pink at pH 2–6, light purple at pH 7, blue at pH 10, and green at pH 12. Interestingly, the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% film exhibited deeper and more noticeable color transformations (shifting from reddish-purple to blue/green) compared to the Car/SPA film. This was probably because the stronger electrostatic interactions between rich-functional groups of Ti-CDs with polymer matrix and SPA improve the adsorption and stability of anthocyanin in polymer chains, thereby intensifying film color changeable ability. The incorporation of 3 wt% of Ti-CDs masks the color of SPA because of the brownish color of carbon dot nanoparticles. However, the color shift of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film in neutral and alkaline pH was still significant and visible compared to the film's color in acidic pH.

The corresponding color parameters (L, a, b, and ΔE) of the indicator films in the pH range of 2–12 are shown in Fig. 7c,d. The high a-value was observed at low pH, indicating the red color of films at low pH. The progressive decline of the a-value towards a negative range as pH rises from 2 to 12 signifies the transition in color from red to green. The b-value varied from positive to negative, affirming the presence of chartreuse hues in the composite films. Additionally, the films displayed higher b-values in the pH range of 10 to 12, signifying a yellowish intensity. As the pH level shifted towards alkaline conditions, the brightness (L-value) of the film decreased owing to the pH-responsive characteristics of SPA. Typically, seafood products exhibit pH levels ranging from 7 to 8 during spoilage [71]. The ΔE values for all three films within the pH range of 6–12 were >5, indicating that the color changes of the indicator films were perceptible to the human eye. Moreover, a ΔE value >12 across the pH range means that the overall color changes can be perceived by individuals without specialized training [72]. These results affirm that Car/SPA/Ti-CD films can act as pH-sensitive indicators for monitoring food quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to ammonia and acid vapors
ce:section.ce:para: Muscle protein breakdown produces volatile compounds, including ammonia, trimethylamine, sulfides, and others. Notably, volatile nitrogen compounds, particularly ammonia, play a significant role in elevating the pH levels of muscle-based food products [67]. Ammonia-responsive colorimetric films can be employed as intelligent packaging materials capable of indicating spoilage in protein-rich foods. The sensitivity of the composite films to ammonia and acid vapors was measured to mimic the fabricated indicator films' response to volatile compounds produced during food sample decay. Fig. 8a illustrates the change in color of the Car/SPA/Ti-CDs film following a 15-min exposure to NH3 and acidic vapor. The color of the indicator films exhibited minimal change upon exposure to volatile acetic acid.

On the other hand, the Car/SPA films presented a visible color change profile from pink to green/yellowish color by the exposure to ammonia within 15 min. The Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films exhibited a distinct color transformation after exposure to ammonia. The initial pink/reddish color of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% film changed to yellow, while the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film shifted to an amber hue. The penetrated ammonia vapors into the polymer matrix became hydrated and formed hydrated ammonia (NH3.H2O). This hydrated ammonia then hydrolyzed, producing ammonium ions (NH4+) and hydroxyl ions (OH−). The presence of NH4+ leads to an alkaline environment on the film's surface, producing betalamic acid that induces the color change of the smart film to green/yellow [68,73].

The color characteristics of the indicator films exposed to the simulated solutions are illustrated in Fig. 8b,c. The decrease in the film's a-value with a simultaneous increase in its b-value provides evidence of a fast shift in the film's hue from pink/reddish to yellow/green due to a reaction with ammonia vapor. In contrast, the color change observed in the composite film when exposed to acetic acid vapor is negligible, suggesting a lower sensitivity to acidic vapors than alkaline vapors. In addition, all the composite films designed exhibited significant changes in ΔE values, resulting in perceptible color changes that could be seen with the unaided eye. The gas sensing function of the engineered film demonstrates its potential for commercial use as a freshness indicator for packaged seafood that experiences an increase in pH levels over time during storage.

Fresh shrimp are prone to microbial spoilage and internal proteolysis over storage, producing volatile alkaline gases [1]. In this context, as the storage time extends, the pH value of shrimp typically rises, which can be detected by films that are sensitive to ammonia [74]. Fig. 9a illustrates the color changes of manufactured films placed within the packaging cup over the 24 h shrimp storage at 25 ± 2 °C. As expected, packaging the shrimp in pure Car film did not result in any observable color alteration. The SPA-added film changed from pink to pale green/yellow during storage. In contrast, the initial pink/reddish color of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films transformed to light and dark yellow/brownish after 1 day. This discoloration represents a critical step in the decomposition of live shrimp and is distinguishable to the naked eye as the intelligent film detects the continuously released and accumulated volatile alkali gases. The color parameters of the indicator (Fig. 9b, c) and the TVB-N and pH values of the samples (Table S1) were also measured at various intervals (0, 12, and 24 h) to assess the shrimp freshness more precisely. Quantitative determination of color values confirmed significant color shifting corresponding to engineered indicator films (Fig. 9b, c). The a-value dropped sharply, and the b-value increased during shrimp spoilage. The color difference (ΔE) of the films during storage surpassed 5, signifying that even an unskilled panellist can observe this film color transition. The pH and TVB-N values of shrimp on day 0 were 6.1 and 7.6 mg/100 g. The TVB-N value of shrimp gradually increased and exceeded the acceptability limit (30 mg/100 g) [22] for edible shrimp at 24 h, indicating that shrimp became inedible. The shrimp's pH progressively rises over storage time from 6.1 to 7.9 due to the presence of volatile nitrogenous compounds generated by enzymes and microorganism-derived protein degradation [75]. The TVB-N and pH alteration in shrimps were consistent with the color-changing profile of the developed sensor, demonstrating the analytical potential of the developed indicator films for rapid and visual assessment of real-time seafood quality.

This study aimed to create Car-based active and intelligent films that can effectively maintain the quality of protein-based products by continuously monitoring their freshness in real-time. Shrimp samples were wrapped with Car-based films while stored at 4 °C for 15 days. Every 5 days, the pH, color parameters, and total viable bacteria count (TVC) of samples are recorded to examine the relationship between these spoilage factors and the visual characteristics of both shrimps and films (Fig. 10 a-d). For the control group (shrimps wrapped with Car film), the color of the shrimp starts to become red partly on the 5th day, indicating the shrimps begin to become inedible. It can be seen that the color of the shrimp packed with Car/SPA was turning red on the 5th day, and the color change of the film started, which is consistent with the color response of the indicator film. At the same time, no observable change in the color of shrimps and films is displayed for the samples covered with Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% for up to 5 days, indicating the engineered films could retarded the spoilage and preserved the quality of packaged food. However, the discoloration of Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films as the indicator film gradually started after 10 days, representing the decay occurrence of the shrimps. The color of Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% film and the appearance of shrimp in this group demonstrate the great preservation performance of the film containing SPA and 3 wt% of Ti-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 10b, the initial pH value of fresh shrimp samples was 5.95 ± 0.03, and the pH rose to 8.45 ± 0.01 and 8.15 ± 0.02 at 15 days for shrimps wrapped with Car and Car/SPA films, respectively, indicating that the shrimp were not acceptable for consumption. Nevertheless, the pH level of shrimps wrapped with Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films were 7.4 ± 0.01 and 6.9 ± 0.01 at 15 days, which is less than the standard limit for spoiled shrimp (7.7) [71] even at long storage period. Moreover, the count of microorganisms is another significant factor influencing seafood quality. Fig. 10c shows a progressive increase in the TVC observed for all shrimp samples in the storage time. The TVC of shrimp samples was 1.95 ± 0.1 Log CFU/mL, which increased to 9.4 ± 0.2 and 8.3 ± 0.1 Log CFU/mL within 15 days for the control and Car/SPA, respectively. On the other hand, the shrimps packaged in Car/SPA/Ti-CD1% and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3% films didn't exceed the maximum allowable limit value of 7.0 Log CFU/mL [76], even after the 15 days of storage. The volatile nitrogen compounds generated by microbial activity increase the pH level, making a correlation between the trend of pH and TVC that indicates the shrimp spoilage process has begun.

A color change from grey to yellow/red after spoilage was also observed in the control shrimp and Car/SPA packaging groups after 5 days. Nevertheless, for the other treatments, the color shift transitioned gradually from grey to a faint yellow tone over the storage period. The degree of spoilage of the shrimp in the different treatment groups was investigated by measuring the a-value (redness) of the shrimp after unpacking (Fig. 10d). The a-value of shrimp wrapped with the Car and Car/SPA films quickly increased within 5 days. However, the a-values of shrimps wrapped with CDs-added films gradually increased after 10 days. The combined antioxidant and antibacterial properties of SPA and Ti-CDs could lead to prolonged shelf life for shrimp. These findings confirm that the Car/SPA/Ti-CDs indicator films, as designed, offer a highly effective packaging material for real-time freshness monitoring and preservation. The experimental results verify the efficacy of the proposed Car/SPA/Ti-CD films based on the results obtained at the lab scale. However, in order to be implemented on a commercial scale, careful investigation is required under various conditions such as time, temperature, and consumer safety. Additionally, the efficacy of the proposed materials needs to be verified on other fresh food models other than shrimp.

Carrageenan-based multifunctional active and intelligent indicator films were prepared using Ti-CDs and anthocyanins from sweet potato peels, a food processing waste. The added fillers showed high compatibility with the carrageenan film, and their addition increased the physicochemical and mechanical properties of the film. The carrageenan film incorporated with the Ti-CDs showed UV-barrier, antioxidant, and antibacterial functional properties. The visual color response of the film to various pH buffers from 2 to 12 and volatile ammonia revealed the high sensitivity of the developed indicator film. The Car/SPA/Ti-CD films perfectly differentiated fresh and spoiled shrimp according to the color change from pink/red to yellow/brown of the indicator observed with the naked eye. While storing shrimp, the film containing Ti-CDs showed good potential as an active food packaging material that can effectively preserve the quality of shrimp. To address the demand for sustainable, active, and intelligent packaging, Car/SPA/Ti-CD films are expected to realize high-value utilization of functional fillers obtained from biomass. The experimental results also suggest that using natural pigments and CD derived from kitchen waste is a creative tool for designing smart and sustainable packaging systems for the future food industry.
